Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 20. The new upper round bound is round 25; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 25; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Stateful reviewer's report

## Referee report on the revised `answer.tex`

### Verdict

The revised draft substantially addresses the earlier objections. I now find the proof mathematically complete at the level expected for a research-style solution relying on DKEP and CDHJKN as black boxes. The previously problematic bridge between relative boundary pieces and the compact boundary has been reworked with enough detail: it uses DKEP’s normal-bidual support formalism, DKEP’s mixing correspondence criterion, CDHJKN’s fusion rules, and an ordinary-bidual limiting argument to avoid the nonmultiplicativity issue.

### LaTeX contract

The displayed source satisfies the First Proof LaTeX contract:

- Uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- Uses `fullpage`, which is permitted.
- No `geometry`, manual margin changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes.
- No apparent undefined macros or cross-reference-breaking syntax.
- The author reports a successful two-pass `pdflatex` compile to 6 pages, which is consistent with the source.

I find no LaTeX-contract reason to reject the answer.

### Literature validation

The revised proof’s main citations now line up with the cited literature:

- DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives the simultaneous-normal bidual framework and embeds the simultaneous biduals into the multiplier bidual setting; this supports the support-corner bookkeeping when combined with the standard support-projection formalism. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Proposition 2.4 and Corollary 3.2 justify testing strong bimodule closure by simultaneous normal functionals, and DKEP Proposition 3.6 identifies \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closures of both \(X\) and \(K_X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Example 3.4 defines the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\), and Example 5.11 identifies the coefficient boundary for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence with that boundary. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9 support the mixing/correspondence arguments and the passage from mixing operators to compact coefficient membership. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 give exactly the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule used in the support-intersection lemma. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion and the u.c.p. map from \(B(L^2M)\) to the compact normal corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives the diffuse full factor conclusion for join-irreducible graph products with at least three vertices and state-zero vertex unitaries. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

### Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly interprets irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This is a faithful and standard interpretation of the problem statement. No issue remains here.

#### Support bookkeeping

The previous draft’s support bookkeeping was too abrupt. The current draft gives a clearer route:

1. Work with ordinary open supports \(r_Y\subset A^{**}\).
2. Use DKEP’s simultaneous-normal bidual construction to identify the normal corner.
3. Use Proposition 2.4, Corollary 3.2, and Proposition 3.6 to show that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same simultaneous-normal annihilator, hence the same compressed support.

This now sufficiently explains why approximate units from the original boundary \(X\) may be used to approximate the DKEP support \(q_X\). This was one of the central earlier gaps, and it is now addressed.

Minor note: the support formula is still written compactly, but the cited DKEP framework plus the argument supplied in the proof is enough for this level of solution.

#### Subalgebra fusion lemma

The previous draft did not adequately justify the coefficient-boundary identification for \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\). The revision fixes this by avoiding a naive elementary-tensor density argument and instead using DKEP Theorem 5.10.

The proof now correctly uses:

- \(H={}_NL^2N_B\),
- DKEP Example 5.11 for the left boundary \(X_B\),
- the full boundary \(B(L^2B)\) on the \(B\)-side,
- the fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\),
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 to pass from cyclic elementary tensors to all bounded vectors,
- DKEP Proposition 5.9 to obtain coefficient membership in \(K_{X_B}(N)\).

This addresses the earlier objection that arbitrary bounded coefficients were not controlled.

The statement that \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_B\) is also acceptable: \(e_B\) is the coefficient of the cyclic vector \(\widehat1\otimes_B\widehat1\), and DKEP Example 3.4 identifies \(X_B\) as the hereditary algebra generated by \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-translates of the Jones projection/corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Finite fusion calculus

The previous direct-sum and finite-fusion lemma was underproved. The new version is much stronger and now explicitly uses condition (1) of DKEP Theorem 5.10. The direct-sum argument via the union of cyclic sets is valid, and the fusion argument using \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) is valid.

The product formula

\[
c_1c_2
=
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{*}T_\beta
\]

is correctly oriented under the DKEP convention \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). The use of a finite direct sum to place the two coefficient vectors in a common correspondence is also appropriate.

The approximate-unit construction for \(X_{M_U}\) from finite positive sums of coefficients and functional calculus is standard and now adequately justified.

#### Boundary-support intersection lemma

This was the central technical weakness in the earlier drafts. The revision now gives a coherent and rigorous proof.

The key chain is:

1. Finite-fusion coefficient products \(hkh\) decompose into coefficients of finite fusions whose labels include both \(W\) and \(U\).
2. CDHJKN’s fusion rule decomposes those fusions into direct sums of \(H_C\) with \(C\subset U\cap W\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
3. The direct-sum/fusion lemma puts those coefficients in \(K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}(M)\).
4. The limiting argument is performed in the ordinary bidual \(B(L^2M)^{**}\), not in the normal bidual, so the nonmultiplicativity issue is avoided.
5. Only after obtaining \(r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}\) is the result compressed by the simultaneous normal support \(p\).

This proves

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

The induction to the palindromic product is then valid.

#### Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This section is now acceptable. The state-compression argument is correct, and the GNS argument using the palindromic positive contraction is careful about the fact that \(\widetilde S(N)\) is only an operator system. The use of DKEP Lemma 8.5’s u.c.p. map into the compact normal corner is supported by the cited source. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The AFP reduced-word argument is now detailed enough. The commutator formula with \(p\) and \(q\), the orthogonality \(q w_1^*w_2q=0\), the inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q,
\]

and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) are all valid.

The invocation of DKEP Lemma 6.1 to place \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\), and DKEP Theorem 6.2 to conclude relative proper proximality, is appropriate. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

#### Graph-product application

The graph-product AFP decomposition is standard from the reduced-word model. The local graph combinatorics are correct. I also brute-force checked the two combinatorial claims for all graphs with \(3\le |V|\le 6\): connected complement implies \(C(v)\neq\varnothing\), and if \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then there is \(s\in\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) with \(s\not\sim r\). No counterexample was found.

The reduced-word expectation computations for \(w_i\) and \(w_1^*w_2\) are valid.

The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is correct: for a join-irreducible graph with at least three vertices and trace-zero vertex unitaries, the graph product is a diffuse full factor. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Since the graph product is separable, diffuse amenability would imply hyperfiniteness, and the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor is not full; hence \(M\) is nonamenable.

### Summary of previous concerns

Resolved:

- The normal-support bookkeeping is now tied to DKEP’s simultaneous-normal bidual framework.
- The equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is now justified through annihilators and strong bimodule closure.
- The subalgebra fused correspondence is handled using DKEP Theorem 5.10 rather than elementary-vector density alone.
- Direct sums and finite fusions are now treated via the cyclic-vector criterion in DKEP Theorem 5.10.
- The boundary-support intersection is now proved in the ordinary bidual before compression.
- The Bass–Serre reduced-word argument has been expanded sufficiently.
- The final graph-product factor/nonamenability step is properly supported by CDHJKN Theorem 2.4.

Remaining issues:

- I do not find any remaining gap that affects mathematical validity. Some arguments are concise and rely on standard expert familiarity with DKEP’s support formalism, but they are no longer merely asserted without a viable route.

New issues introduced:

- None affecting correctness.

## Independent fresh reviewer's report

## Referee report on `answer.tex`

### LaTeX contract check

I compiled `answer.tex` with `pdflatex` twice. It compiles successfully and produces a 6-page PDF, within the 12-page limit. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, `fullpage` is the only layout package, and I found no forbidden font-size or spacing commands. Thus I do **not** reject it on LaTeX-contract grounds.

### Source validation summary

The cited DKEP and CDHJKN papers exist and contain many of the broad tools invoked. For example, DKEP Example 3.4 defines the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\) using operators with arbitrary middle term \(T\in e_B\mathbb B(L^2M)e_B\), and identifies it as a hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\)-type operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.10 gives the equivalence between a cyclic bounded-vector condition and relative mixing/relative compactness of all bounded vectors. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives the bidual characterization of relative proper proximality under an ergodic-center action hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 do give the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in simplified form in the proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 also supports the final assertion that join-irreducible graph products with at least three vertices and trace-zero vertex unitaries are diffuse full factors. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

However, several invocations in `answer.tex` are substantially stronger than what is explicitly established in the cited sources, or require additional bridging lemmas that are not proved. These gaps affect core steps of the proof.

---

## Main mathematical issues

### 1. The support-bookkeeping lemma is not rigorously established

The lemma claims that for every hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-invariant subalgebra \(Y\subset \mathbb B(L^2N)\),
\[
r_Yp=pr_Y,\qquad (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y),
\]
and then identifies \(pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X\).

This is an essential technical bridge, because later the proof multiplies ordinary open supports in \(\mathbb B(L^2M)^{**}\) and then compresses by \(p\). But the proof only sketches this from DKEP Section 2.3 and Proposition 2.10. DKEP Proposition 2.10 states an inclusion/intersection result for simultaneous normal biduals and nonunital inclusion of \((B^{\sharp_J})^*\) into \((A^{\sharp_J})^*\); it does not, as stated, provide the ordinary-open-support commutation and corner formula asserted here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This is not a harmless omission. The later inequality
\[
q_Wq_Uq_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_I}p=q_I
\]
depends exactly on this support formula. Without a fully proved lemma connecting ordinary bidual supports to DKEP’s simultaneous normal-bidual supports, the central intersection argument is not justified.

### 2. Equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is underproved

The proof asserts that \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\)-functionals annihilating \(X\) are exactly those annihilating \(K_X(N)\), using DKEP Proposition 2.4 and Proposition 3.6. DKEP Proposition 3.6 does identify
\[
K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
=\overline{K_X(N)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
\]
and shows this is a strong bimodule. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But turning this into equality of the **compressed support projections** again relies on the unproved support-corner formula above. The argument is plausible, but it is not written at a level sufficient for a standalone proof.

### 3. The coefficient identification for \(X_B\) is incomplete

In Lemma “subalgebra fusion”, the proof says that since \(e_B\) is a coefficient of \(\zeta_B\), and DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) from translates of \(e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B\), it follows that \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_B\).

This is too fast. DKEP Example 3.4 includes arbitrary operators \(T\in e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B\), not just the projection \(e_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) To justify the claim, one must prove that the hereditary algebra generated by positive coefficients of
\[
\mathcal H_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N
\]
indeed contains all operators
\[
x_1Jy_1J\,T\,Jy_2Jx_2,\qquad T\in e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B.
\]
The present proof does not show how the whole middle corner \(e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B\cong \mathbb B(L^2B)\) is generated by these coefficients. This gap propagates directly into Lemma `finite fusion calculus`, where approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\) are built from coefficient generators.

### 4. The use of DKEP Theorem 5.10 for \(\mathcal H_B\) is not fully checked

The proof says that elementary bounded tensors \(\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta\) are cyclic in \(\mathcal H_B\) and satisfy
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta}=T_{\bar\eta}T_\xi\in X_B\,\mathbb B(L^2N)\,X_B,
\]
so Theorem 5.10 applies. DKEP Theorem 5.10 requires a precise cyclic set of bounded vectors satisfying the relative boundary condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The answer does not verify the boundedness conventions, the exact endpoint boundary pieces, or the domain/codomain compatibility of the \(T\)-operators. These are technical but essential details in this framework.

### 5. The finite-fusion coefficient algebra \(\mathcal C_U\) is not rigorously proved to be a \(*\)-algebra

The identity
\[
c_1c_2
=
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta
\tag{2.1}
\]
is central to the claim that \(\mathcal C_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra. But the proof does not spell out the precise convention under which the two vectors in the displayed coefficient live in a common correspondence. The phrase “viewed in the finite direct sum \((K\otimes_N\bar K\otimes_N L)\oplus L\)” is not sufficient: off-diagonal coefficients in direct sums require careful treatment, and depending on the \(T_\xi\)-operator convention, the displayed expression may not be literally meaningful as a coefficient of a single correspondence.

Because the construction of approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\) and the intersection lemma both rely on \(\mathcal C_U\) being a \(*\)-algebra, this is an essential gap.

### 6. The boundary-support intersection lemma depends on the preceding unproved machinery

The proof of
\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
is the technical core of the whole solution. CDHJKN does provide the relevant graph-product fusion rule in a form that supports the intended strategy. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) But the proof of the intersection lemma also needs:

1. the support-bookkeeping lemma;
2. the coefficient identification \(X_{M_U}=X_{H_U}\) in the strong enough sense used;
3. the \(*\)-algebra property of \(\mathcal C_U\);
4. the assertion that coefficients of arbitrary direct sums of relevant fusions land in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\);
5. the monotonicity \(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\Rightarrow K_{X_{M_C}}\subset K_{X_{M_I}}\), which is used but not explicitly justified.

Since several of these are not fully proved, the intersection lemma is not established rigorously.

### 7. The finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma is plausible but still has missing details

The argument in Lemma `finite` is mostly coherent. Still, two details need proof:

- When compressing a central state by \(q_i^\perp\), the answer says the restriction to \(N\) is a nonzero central normal positive functional. This is true if one proves domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\), but the proof only states it.
- The use of the u.c.p. map
  \[
  \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
  \]
  is attributed to DKEP Lemma 8.5. DKEP does mention natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps into the normal-bidual compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But the exact formula \(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\) and the verification that composing with \(\omega\) gives a Connes hypertrace should be written out more carefully, especially because the canonical map into the normal bidual is not being treated as multiplicative elsewhere.

These are repairable, but they are still gaps in a standalone proof.

### 8. The Bass–Serre proposition is the strongest part, but still somewhat terse

The AFP reduced-word argument for the projections \(p,q\) is plausible. DKEP Lemma 6.1 supports reducing membership in \(S_X(M)\) to checking commutators on a generating set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The paradoxical inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
also look correct under the stated centeredness assumptions.

However, the proof only sketches the right-multiplication commutator computation and does not explicitly check all adjoint generators required by DKEP Lemma 6.1. This is less serious than the support/fusion gaps above, but still not fully polished.

### 9. The graph-product application is mostly correct conditional on the earlier lemmas

The graph combinatorics used to choose \(w_1,w_2\) appear correct. The final use of CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 to obtain that \(M\) is a diffuse full factor is supported by the cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) The conclusion that such a factor is nonamenable is standard.

One minor missing justification is the assertion
\[
M_\emptyset=\mathbb C,\qquad X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M).
\]
This is true, but the answer does not prove it. It is not the main obstruction.

---

## Overall verdict

The proof has a plausible global strategy, and many individual components are likely repairable. But in its present form, `answer.tex` does **not** give a complete rigorous solution. The decisive unresolved issues are the unproved normal-bidual support calculus and the insufficiently justified coefficient/fusion machinery used to prove the boundary-support intersection lemma. Since that intersection lemma is essential for upgrading finitely many relative boundary results to ordinary proper proximality, the current document is not answer-ready.

# Let's construct revised answer as a full string.
new = r'''\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cT}{\mathcal T}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible means not a non-trivial graph join; equivalently the complement
graph \(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the notation of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.
For a finite algebra \(N\), put \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*,
\]
the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual.  If \(X\) is an
\(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\) is DKEP's compact hereditary algebra and
\(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support of \((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For
\(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Set
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the absence of an \(N\)-central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is normal.

For a bounded vector \(\xi\) in an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence, \(T_\xi\) denotes
DKEP's coefficient operator \(L^2M\to L^2N\); if \(\eta\) is bounded in an
\(N\)-\(P\) correspondence, then
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes_N\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad
        T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^* .                                    \tag{1}
\]

\section*{Boundary supports and coefficients}

Let \(A=A_N\) and let \(p\in A^{**}\) be the simultaneous normal support
projection, so that \(\cA_N=pA^{**}p\).  If \(Y\subset A\) is hereditary and
invariant under both multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), let
\(r_Y\in A^{**}\) be its ordinary open support.

\begin{lemma}[support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
For such \(Y\),
\[
        r_Yp=pr_Y,\qquad
        (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\cA_N(pr_Y).
\]
If \((e_\lambda)\) is a contractive approximate unit of \(Y\), then
\(e_\lambda\to r_Y\) strongly* in \(A^{**}\), and \(pe_\lambda p\to pr_Y\)
ultra-strongly* in \(\cA_N\).  If \(X\) is a boundary piece with
\(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\), then
\[
        pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For one normal multiplier action, DKEP Section 2.3 identifies the normal
bidual with \(p_1A^{**}p_1\).  If \(B\subset A\) is invariant and hereditary,
then the universal normal representation of \(B\) is the restriction of the
universal normal representation of \(A\) to the reducing subspace with support
\(r_B\); equivalently, inside \(M(A)^{**}\),
\[
        (B^\sharp)^*=r_Bp_1A^{**}p_1r_B
\]
and \(r_Bp_1=p_1r_B\).  Applying this to the two commuting actions and using
DKEP Proposition 2.10, which realizes the simultaneous normal bidual as the
intersection of the two one-normal biduals and gives the corresponding
non-unital inclusion for invariant subalgebras, gives the displayed corner
formula.  The convergence of approximate units is the usual strong* convergence
to the open support, compressed by \(p\).

DKEP Proposition 2.4 says that simultaneous-normal functionals vanish on a
subspace precisely when they vanish on its simultaneous strong bimodule
closure.  By DKEP Corollary 3.2 and Proposition 3.6,
\[
        \overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
        =\overline{K_X(N)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}},
\]
and this common space is the simultaneous strong closure of both \(X\) and
\(K_X(N)\).  Thus \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same annihilator in
\(A_N^{\sharp_J}\).  In the corner description above, the annihilator of
\((pr_Y)\cA_N(pr_Y)\) is exactly the functionals supported on
\(1-pr_Y\); hence equal annihilators give equal compressed supports.  This is
\(pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X\).
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
The vector \(\zeta_B=\widehat 1\otimes_B\widehat 1\) satisfies
\(T_{\zeta_B}^*T_{\zeta_B}=e_B\).

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra fusion]\label{lem:subalg}
The DKEP boundary piece \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by positive
coefficients of \(\cH_B\).  Moreover \(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\), and every
bounded coefficient of every direct sum of copies of \(\cH_B\) is in
\(K_{X_B}(N)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Example 3.4 identifies \(X_B\) with the hereditary subalgebra generated by
the operators \(xJyJe_B\), \(x,y\in N\).  Since
\(e_B=T_{\zeta_B}^*T_{\zeta_B}\), the positive coefficients of the vectors
\(x\zeta_By\) are
\[
        xJyJ\,e_B\,Jy^*Jx^* .
\]
The hereditary algebra generated by these positive coefficients contains
\((xJyJe_B)A_N(e_BJy'Jx')\), hence the whole algebraic span
\(x_1Jy_1JTJy_2Jx_2\), \(T\in e_BA_Ne_B\), appearing in Example 3.4; the
reverse inclusion is immediate.  This proves the first assertion.

Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\).  DKEP Example 5.11 and Theorem 5.10 say that \(H\) is
mixing relative to \(X_B\) on the \(N\)-side and to the full boundary
\(\mathbb B(L^2B)\) on the \(B\)-side; the conjugate correspondence has the
opposite relative boundaries.  For the cyclic bounded tensors
\(\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta\) in \(H\otimes_B\bar H=\cH_B\), (1) gives
\(T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta}=T_{\bar\eta}T_\xi\in
X_B\,\mathbb B(L^2N)\,X_B\).  The cyclic-vector criterion in DKEP Theorem 5.10
therefore applies to \(\cH_B\), and every bounded vector \(\alpha\in\cH_B\)
has \(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B,X_B}(N,N)\).  DKEP Proposition 5.9 gives
\(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\in K_{X_B}(N)\).  Direct sums are handled by taking the
union of the cyclic sets in the summands.  Finally,
\(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes_BJ\xi\) gives
\(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite fusion calculus]\label{lem:fusion}
Let \(X\) be a boundary piece for \(N\), with \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\).  Finite
Connes fusions, conjugates, and arbitrary direct sums of \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondences which are mixing relative to \(X\times X\) are again mixing
relative to \(X\times X\).  Consequently, if \(K\) is any such finite-fusion
correspondence and \(\xi\in K\) is bounded, then \(T_\xi\in K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), put \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).  Let \(\cT_U\) be the
linear span of all \(T_\xi\), where \(\xi\) is a bounded vector in a finite
direct sum of non-empty finite fusions of \(H_U\) and \(\overline{H_U}\).
Then \(\cT_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra, and \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive
approximate unit in the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For direct sums the cyclic set in DKEP Theorem 5.10(1) is the union of the
cyclic sets in the summands.  For a fusion, elementary tensors from the two
cyclic sets are cyclic and satisfy (1); since \(X\) is hereditary,
\(X\mathbb B(L^2N)X\subset X\).  Conjugates are handled by taking adjoints in
(1).  Theorem 5.10 then gives \(T_\xi\in K_{X,X}(N,N)\) for every bounded
\(\xi\).  By Proposition 5.9, \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) and \(T_\xi T_\xi^*\) are in
the hereditary algebra \(K_X(N)\), hence \(T_\xi\in K_X(N)\).

The algebra property of \(\cT_U\) is now just (1):
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\), and
\(T_\xi^*=T_{\bar\xi}\); direct sums absorb finite sums.  By Lemma
\ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) of \(H_U\), which are among the \(t^*t\)'s.
For a finite positive sum \(s\) of such generators,
\(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) belongs to the hereditary algebra
generated by \(s\), and the net over \(s\) and \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\) is a
contractive approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\).  Polynomial approximation to
\(g_\varepsilon^{1/2}\), with zero constant term, puts \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) in
the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\), hence \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\)
and, by monotonicity of the DKEP compact construction, \(K_{X_{M_Z}}(M)\subset
K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .                                  \tag{2}
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^m U_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule of
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} give
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction shows that any finite fusion with
labels \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes as a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).

Let \(h\) and \(k\) first be finite sums of \(t^*t\)'s with
\(t\in\cT_W\) and \(t\in\cT_U\), respectively.  Expanding \(hkh\) gives a
finite sum of monomials in \(T_\xi\)'s and adjoints whose labels include both
\(W\) and \(U\).  By (1) each monomial is \(T_\zeta\) for a bounded vector
\(\zeta\) in a finite fusion of the corresponding \(H_W\)'s and \(H_U\)'s.
By (3) this fusion is a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset I\).  Lemma
\ref{lem:fusion} and monotonicity give
\[
        hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M).                                   \tag{4}
\]
Norm closedness gives (4) for \(h,k\) in the closures of those positive cones.

Now take limits in the ordinary bidual \(A=\mathbb B(L^2M)^{**}\), where the
canonical copy of \(\mathbb B(L^2M)\) is multiplicative.  Choose contractive
approximate units \(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and \(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\)
from the positive cones above.  If \(r_R\) denotes the ordinary support of
\(X_{M_R}\), and \(r_{K_I}\) that of \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), then
\(h_\alpha\to r_W\) and \(k_\beta\to r_U\) strongly*.  By (4),
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in r_{K_I}Ar_{K_I}\).  This corner is strongly*
closed, so first letting \(\beta\) and then \(\alpha\) tend to infinity gives
\(r_Wr_Ur_W\in r_{K_I}Ar_{K_I}\).  Since it is a positive contraction,
\(0\le r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}\).  Compressing by the simultaneous normal support
\(p\) and using Lemma \ref{lem:support},
\[
        q_Wq_Uq_W=pr_W\,pr_U\,pr_W=p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p\le pr_{K_I}p=q_I .
\]
The palindromic assertion follows by induction from (2).
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{5}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and put \(q=q_i\).
Since \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\); hence \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\).
If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), then
\[
        y\mapsto \omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)
\]
is an \(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  On \(N_+\) it is
dominated by \(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\), hence is normal; since \(N\) is a
factor and the state is \(N\)-central, its restriction to \(N\) is \(\tau\).
This contradicts relative proper proximality.  Thus \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\)
for all \(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state on \(\cA_N\).  In the GNS representation each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, so \(\omega(R)=1\).  The element \(R\) is a
positive contraction in \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\); hence \(0\le R\le q_0\) and
\(\omega(q_0)=1\).  DKEP Lemma 8.5 supplies the canonical \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\cA_Nq_0
\]
which sends \(a\in N\) to \(aq_0\).  Then
\(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is \(N\)-central by bimodularity, and
\(\psi(a)=\omega(aq_0)=\omega(a)=\tau(a)\) because \(q_0\) fixes the GNS cyclic
vector.  Thus \(\psi\) is a Connes hypertrace on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\),
contradicting non-amenability of the finite factor \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal form
is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{n\ge1,\ i_1\ne\cdots\ne i_n}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n},\quad i_j\in\{P,Q\}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) project onto the summands whose first letter is \(P\),
respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  If \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x^*\) preserves the first letter except that \(L^2B\) is
sent to a one-letter \(P\)-word and a one-letter \(P\)-word may acquire an
\(L^2B\)-component.  Checking the three classes \(L^2B\), first-letter \(P\),
and first-letter \(Q\),
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{6}
\]
The two terms in (6) are \(N,JNJ\)-translates of \(e_B\), hence lie in \(X_B\).
The same computation with \(P\) and \(Q\) interchanged handles
\(x\in Q\ominus B\); adjoints are included because these centered subspaces are
self-adjoint, and elements of \(B\) commute with \(p,q\).  Since these
subspaces generate \(N\), DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) whose restriction to
\(N\) is normal.  For \(\eta\in qL^2N\), left multiplication by a centered
element of \(P\) prepends a \(P\)-letter, so \(w_iqL^2N\subset pL^2N\).  Also
\(w_1^*w_2\in P\ominus B\), and such an element sends first-letter \(Q\) words
into first-letter \(P\) words; hence \(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\).  Thus
\[
        w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad        a p a^*\le q .
\]
Centrality gives \(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)\), so
\(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power is an alternating
reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, so \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  Hence \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and have the same
\(\varphi\)-value; thus \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  This contradicts
\(1=\varphi(e_B+p+q)\).  By DKEP Theorem 6.2, \(N\) is properly proximal
relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph-product reduced-word
model gives the standard amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{7}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then
connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) give \(s\in L_v\) with
\(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); otherwise \(\{v,r\}\) would be a component of
\(\Gamma^c\).  Set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both cases the graph-product
reduced-word calculus gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (7), so
Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_i L_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} puts the associated
palindromic product in \(q_\emptyset\cA_Mq_\emptyset\).  Here
\(M_\emptyset=\C\), \(e_\C\) is the rank-one projection onto
\(\C\widehat1\), and DKEP Example 3.4 gives \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb
K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor.  A
diffuse amenable finite factor is the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor and is not
full, so \(M\) is non-amenable.  It is separable because the graph is finite
and the vertices are separable.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the finite
family \(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{DKEP}
C.~Ding, S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and J.~Peterson,
\emph{Properly proximal von Neumann algebras},
Duke Math. J. \textbf{172} (2023), 2821--2894.

\bibitem{CDHJKN}
I.~Charlesworth, R.~de Santiago, B.~Hayes, D.~Jekel,
S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and B.~Nelson,
\emph{On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras},
Publ. Res. Inst. Math. Sci. \textbf{61} (2025), 713--762.

\bibitem{CF17}
M.~Caspers and P.~Fima,
\emph{Graph products of operator algebras},
J. Noncommut. Geom. \textbf{11} (2017), 367--411.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}

notes_path="/mnt/data/file-JupXXKWKrSdzvB32VK2ytg-research_notes.tex"
notes=Path(notes_path).read_text()
insert = r"""

\section*{Normal-support and \(T_\xi\)-operator calculus}

For an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence \(H\), DKEP use bounded-vector operators
\(T_\xi\in B(L^2M,L^2N)\).  The crucial functorial rules are
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes_N\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*.
\]
Thus, for an \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence, operators \(T_\xi\) attached to
bounded vectors in finite fusions form a concrete multiplicative calculus:
products are represented by fusing the underlying bounded vectors, and adjoints
by conjugating the correspondence.  It is safer to use the linear span of these
operators, rather than the span of ordinary coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\),
when proving a star-algebra statement.  Ordinary positive coefficients are
still of the form \(t^*t\) with \(t=T_\xi\).

For a subalgebra boundary \(X_B\), Example 3.4 says that \(X_B\) is the
hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\).  Since \(e_B\) is the positive
coefficient of \(\widehat1\otimes_B\widehat1\in
L^2N_B\otimes_B L^2N\), positive coefficients of the translates
\(x(\widehat1\otimes\widehat1)y\) generate all \(xJyJe_B\): the hereditary
algebra contains \(e_BAe_B\), and if \(z=xJyJe_B\), then \(zz^*\) is one of
these positive coefficients while \(z^*z\in e_BAe_B\).  Hence it contains
\(z\) and therefore all \(zTz'\) with \(T\in e_BAe_B\), which is the algebraic
span in Example 3.4.

For the boundary-support intersection, approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\) can
be chosen from the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\mathcal T_U\}\), where
\(\mathcal T_U\) is the span of \(T_\xi\)'s for bounded vectors in non-empty
finite fusions of \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) and its conjugate.  If
\(h\) is built from \(\mathcal T_W\) and \(k\) from \(\mathcal T_U\), every
monomial in the expansion of \(hkh\) is a single \(T_\zeta\) for a finite
fusion whose label list includes both \(W\) and \(U\).  CDHJKN's fusion rule
then decomposes it into \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset W\cap U\); DKEP Theorem 5.10
and Proposition 5.9 put these \(T_\zeta\)'s in \(K_{X_{M_{W\cap U}}}(M)\).

For the support bookkeeping, the working principle is: if \(Y\subset
B(L^2N)\) is hereditary and invariant under the two multiplier actions, then
DKEP's simultaneous-normal bidual includes \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) as the corner
cut out by the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) compressed by the simultaneous
normal support \(p\).  Hence \(q_Y=pr_Y\).  The equality \(q_X=q_{K_X}\) follows
because \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same simultaneous strong closure
\(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), so they have the same simultaneous-normal annihilator.
"""

else:
